content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 1 - Part 5 - The changing marketing landscape
Chapter 1 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Part 5 - The changing marketing landscape

Learning Objective 5 – we will learn to >>>
1.5 Describe the major trends and forces that are changing the marketing landscape in this age of relationships.

The changing marketing landscape (1 of 9)
The digital age: Online, mobile and social media marketing
The consumer love affair with digital and mobile technology makes it fertile ground for marketers trying to engage customers.
Digital and social media marketing involves using digital marketing tools, such as websites, social media, mobile ads and apps, online video, email, blogs and other digital platforms, to engage consumers anywhere, anytime via their computers, smartphones, tablets, Smart TVs and other digital devices.

The changing marketing landscape (2 of 9)
The digital age: Online, mobile and social media marketing (cont.)
Social media marketing
Most brand websites, and even traditional media ads, feature links to the brand’s Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, LinkedIn, YouTube, Snapchat or other social media sites.
Online social media offer an ideal platform for real-time marketing by which marketers can engage consumers in the moment by linking brands to important trending topics, real-world events, causes, personal occasions or other important happenings in consumers’ lives.

The changing marketing landscape (3 of 9)
The digital age: Online, mobile and social media marketing (cont.)
Mobile marketing
Mobile marketing is perhaps the fastest-growing digital marketing platform.
Four out of five smartphone users use their phones to:
access their bank account for online transfers and payments
browse product information through apps or the mobile web
make in-store price comparisons
read online product reviews
find and redeem coupons.

The changing marketing landscape (4 of 9)
Mobile marketing (cont.)
Smartphones are:
ever-present
always on
finely targeted
highly personal.
This makes smartphones ideal for engaging customers anytime, anywhere, as they move through the buying process.

The changing marketing landscape (5 of 9)
The digital age: Online, mobile and social media marketing (cont.)
Big data and artificial intelligence (AI)
The explosion in digital technologies means we now have ready access to more data than ever before.
Companies can use this big data to gain deep consumer insights, personalise marketing offers, and improve communication, engagement and service with consumers.

The changing marketing landscape (6 of 9)
Big data and artificial intelligence (AI) (cont.)
To make sense of big data and to be able to use it effectively, marketers are turning to even more-advanced marketing analytics.
Artificial intelligence (AI) involves machines thinking and learning in a way that looks and feels human, but with much greater analytical capacity.
Marketers use AI to analyse data at lightning speed and apply marketing insights to engage customers in real time and help them through the buying process.

The changing marketing landscape (7 of 9)
The growth of not-for-profit marketing
Not-for-profit marketing is a type of marketing practised by a variety of organisations whose aim is to make surpluses so as to continue their operations, but that do not seek to make profits for shareholders.

The changing marketing landscape (8 of 9)
Rapid globalization
Geographical and cultural distances have shrunk as a result of burgeoning air travel, telephone links, digital communication, social networking and other technical advances.
These technological advances have allowed companies to significantly expand their geographical market coverage, purchasing and manufacturing.
The result is a vastly more complex marketing environment for both companies and consumers.
Q: Rapid globalisation brings with it both marketing opportunities and marketing challenges. Give some examples of each.

The changing marketing landscape (9 of 9)
Sustainable marketing: The call for more environmental and social responsibility
There is an increased call for companies to take responsibility for the social and environmental impact of their actions.
Corporate ethics have become an important matter in almost every business arena, from the corporate boardroom to the business-school classroom.
Q: Global companies sometimes move operations from one country to another in search of lower labour costs. Should they be responsible for problems that arise as a result?

So, what is marketing? Pulling it all together
Figure 1.6:
An expanded model of the marketing process

The changing marketing landscape (8 of 9)
Q: Rapid globalisation brings with it both marketing opportunities and marketing challenges. Give some examples of each.
A: Marketing opportunities of globalisation include enabling access to new markets and to new products. The challenges include having new competitors entering the marketer’s “home” market, offering lower prices or newer technology, or simply, “something different”.

The changing marketing landscape (9 of 9)
Q: Global companies sometimes move operations from one country to another in search of lower labour costs. Should they be responsible for problems that arise as a result?
A: This an ethical question. Many Australian fashion brands have signed a world-wide accord agreeing to buy garments only from ethical overseas suppliers.
